skill.name: building-mcp-servers
skill.description: Guides developers through building Model Context Protocol servers for external APIs and services. It covers TypeScript and Python implementations, tool design, authentication, transport choices, Docker deployment, and evaluation creation.
skill.summary: Build MCP servers with practical TypeScript, Python, deployment, and evaluation guidance.
skill.author: Asmayaseen

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many high and critical signals, but most are false positives from Markdown examples and headings. The confirmed risks are expected evaluator capabilities: launching a user-specified local MCP command, connecting to user-specified remote endpoints, and handling user-provided credentials. No evidence found of malicious intent, hidden exfiltration, real hardcoded secrets, weak cryptographic code, or prompt injection attempts.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation Harness Can Launch User-Specified Commands
medium_findings.description: The evaluation workflow accepts a command, arguments, and environment values for stdio MCP servers, then passes them into MCP stdio connection setup. This is expected for evaluating local servers, but it means the script can execute arbitrary user-selected programs if run with untrusted input.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command and arguments are user-controlled CLI values that are forwarded to StdioServerParameters. The behavior is legitimate for an evaluator, so the risk is contextual rather than malicious.

medium_findings.title: Evaluation Harness Can Connect to Arbitrary MCP URLs
medium_findings.description: The evaluation script accepts SSE or HTTP URLs and optional headers, then connects to those endpoints. This is normal for testing remote MCP servers, but users should avoid untrusted endpoints and sensitive headers.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script directly supports user-provided remote URLs and headers. I found no evidence of hidden exfiltration, but remote connection behavior is real and should be disclosed.

medium_findings.title: Documentation Encourages Credential and Token Handling
medium_findings.description: Reference material contains examples for API keys, bearer tokens, forwarded JWTs, and environment variables. These examples are instructional and use placeholders, but users could copy them with real secrets into files or shell history.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly involve credentials, but they are documentation patterns and placeholders rather than hardcoded real secrets. The main risk is unsafe user adaptation.

low_findings.title: Static Analyzer Flagged Markdown Examples as Executable Shell Patterns
low_findings.description: Most external command findings occur in Markdown reference guides and code fences, including install, build, and evaluation commands. They are instructional content rather than code that runs automatically when the skill is loaded.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines are Markdown examples or prose, not runtime execution paths. The actual scripts were reviewed separately for real command behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported weak cryptography blocker locations align with Markdown syntax, headings, version strings, examples, and general text rather than cryptographic implementations. No evidence found of MD5, SHA-1, DES, or similar weak algorithms used by executable skill code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Review of the cited areas found documentation structure and server examples, not cryptographic routines. I did not find executable weak-crypto use in the scripts.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text that claims to override system instructions, skip security review, or impersonate higher-priority instructions. The skill content stays focused on MCP server development guidance.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted search for common prompt-injection phrases returned no matches. Manual review of the main skill instructions also found no override attempt.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Local Process Launch for Stdio Evaluation
dangerous_patterns.description: The stdio evaluation path can start a local MCP server command selected by the user. This is powerful by design and should be used only with trusted commands.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command, args, and env values are accepted through CLI options and passed into the stdio MCP client. This is confirmed runtime behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Controlled Remote Endpoint and Headers
dangerous_patterns.description: The SSE and HTTP evaluation paths can connect to user-specified URLs with user-specified headers. Avoid sending credentials to untrusted MCP endpoints.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code accepts URL and header parameters and forwards them to MCP HTTP/SSE clients. This is intentional for evaluation but still security-relevant.

content.value_statement: Building MCP servers requires clear tool design, secure auth, and reliable evaluation. This skill provides structured guidance, implementation patterns, and test workflows for Python and TypeScript servers.
actual_capabilities: Explains MCP server planning, naming, tool schemas, and response design.
Provides Python FastMCP and TypeScript MCP SDK implementation patterns.
Covers stdio, SSE, and streamable HTTP transport choices.
Includes guidance for authentication, headers, environment variables, and Docker deployment.
Provides scripts and reference material for evaluating MCP servers with test questions.
limitations: Does not build a complete server without service-specific API details.
Requires users to supply their own credentials, endpoints, and authorization model.
Evaluation scripts can call live MCP tools and may incur API costs.
Reference examples must be adapted and reviewed before production use.

use_cases.title: Create an API-backed MCP server
use_cases.description: Plan and implement MCP tools that expose a product API through clear, validated operations.

use_cases.title: Standardize server patterns
use_cases.description: Use shared naming, transport, authentication, and deployment guidance across multiple MCP integrations.

use_cases.title: Evaluate tool quality
use_cases.description: Create read-only evaluation questions and run them against an MCP server to measure agent effectiveness.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a server
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an MCP server for this API. Identify useful tools, required auth, transports, and first implementation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design tool schemas
prompt_templates.prompt: Design MCP tool names, input schemas, output formats, and annotations for these API operations.

prompt_templates.title: Review implementation
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this MCP server implementation for tool quality, auth handling, error messages, pagination, and production readiness.

prompt_templates.title: Create evaluations
prompt_templates.prompt: Create ten independent, read-only evaluation questions for this MCP server, with stable expected answers and verification notes.

output_examples.input: Plan a GitHub MCP server for issue and pull request workflows.
output: A prioritized tool list for repository search, issue triage, pull request review, and metadata lookup.
Authentication and pagination guidance for GitHub API usage.
Evaluation ideas that verify issue counts, labels, assignees, and merged pull requests.

output_examples.input: Review my TypeScript MCP server before deployment.
output: Findings about schema strictness, error handling, transport setup, and missing tool annotations.
Recommended changes for safer authentication, clearer descriptions, and structured output.
A short production checklist covering build, inspector testing, and Docker configuration.

best_practices: Start with real user workflows, then map them to clear MCP tools.
Validate every input and return focused results with pagination where needed.
Run evaluations against read-only tasks before exposing write operations.
anti_patterns: Do not mirror every API endpoint without considering agent workflows.
Do not store real credentials in examples, logs, or evaluation files.
Do not expose destructive tools without clear annotations and authorization checks.

faq.question: Does this skill generate a complete MCP server automatically?
faq.answer: No. It guides implementation and review. You still need service-specific API details and credentials.

faq.question: Which languages does it cover?
faq.answer: It covers TypeScript with the MCP SDK and Python with FastMCP patterns.

faq.question: Can it help with remote MCP servers?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers streamable HTTP, SSE concepts, host validation, Docker notes, and health checks.

faq.question: Does it include evaluation support?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance and scripts for running question-based MCP server evaluations.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production credentials?
faq.answer: Use caution. Pass credentials through approved environment or secret systems and avoid committing them.

faq.question: Can this skill consume existing MCP servers?
faq.answer: No. It is intended for building and evaluating MCP servers, not for regular use of existing servers.
